en.extract:
The Solomons nightjar (Eurostopodus nigripennis) is a species of nightjar in the family Caprimulgidae.
It is found in the Solomon Islands archipelago. Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical dry forests. It avoids human activity, and (like other nightjars) is nocturnal.
It was recently split from the white-throated nightjar based on differences in vocalizations, morphology, and range.
Juvenile plumage is duller than that of adults.
It feeds on flying insects.


Breeding and Nesting
Solomons nightjars typically nest on flat ground 20-30m from the ocean in a mix of leaf litter, sand, and limestone coral, with clutches of a single egg. Eggs are off-white with large blotched speckling. Nestlings are threatened by fire ants and feral cats.

The '''Solomons nightjar''' ('''''Eurostopodus nigripennis''''') is a species of [[nightjar]] in the family [[Caprimulgidae]].
It is found in the [[Solomon Islands archipelago]]. Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical dry [[forest]]s. It avoids human activity,<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Alabai |first1=Maasafi |last2=Esau |first2=Tommy |last3=Kekeubata |first3=Esau |last4=Waneagea |first4=Jackson |last5=MacLaren |first5=David |last6=Major |first6=Richard E. |last7=Callaghan |first7=Corey T. |date=2019-12-16 |title=First record of Solomons Nightjar Eurostopodus nigripennis for Malaita, with a description of its nest site |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |volume=139 |issue=4 |pages=325 |doi=10.25226/bboc.v139i4.2019.a4 |issn=0007-1595|doi-access=free |hdl=1959.4/unsworks_67097 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> and (like other nightjars) is nocturnal.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Guo |first1=Tina V. |last2=Mosah |first2=Stanton |last3=McCullough |first3=Jenna M. |last4=DeRaad |first4=Devon A. |last5=DeCicco |first5=Lucas H. |last6=Tigulu |first6=Ikuo G. |last7=Famoo |first7=Roy |last8=Hobete |first8=Jonathan |last9=Runi |first9=Lazarus |last10=Rusa |first10=Gloria |last11=Tippet |first11=Alan |last12=Ben |first12=Twomey |last13=Olson |first13=Karen V. |last14=Klicka |first14=Lukas B. |last15=Moyle |first15=Robert G. |date=2021-09-14 |title=Detailed description of the nest, eggs, and juvenile plumage of the Solomons Nightjar (Eurostopodus nigripennis) |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1676/1559-4491-132.4.1028 |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=132 |issue=4 |doi=10.1676/1559-4491-132.4.1028 |s2cid=237507282 |issn=1559-4491|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

It was recently split from the white-throated nightjar based on differences in vocalizations, morphology, and range.<ref name=":1" />

Juvenile plumage is duller than that of adults.<ref name=":1" />

It feeds on flying insects.<ref name=":1" />

== Breeding and Nesting ==
Solomons nightjars typically nest on flat ground 20-30m from the ocean in a mix of leaf litter, sand, and limestone coral,<ref name=":1" /> with clutches of a single egg.<ref name=":0" /> Eggs are off-white with large blotched speckling.<ref name=":1" /> Nestlings are threatened by fire ants and feral cats.<ref name=":1" />
